coredata.dc:title: A carbon dots-MnO2 nanosheet-based turn-on pseudochemodosimeter as low-cost probe for selective detection of hazardous mercury ion contaminations in water
coredata.dc:creator: Thakuri, Ankit
Bhosle, Akhil A.
Hiremath, Sharanabasava D.
Banerjee, Mainak
Chatterjee, Amrita
coredata.dc:description: Mercury is a highly hazardous element due to its profound toxicity and wide abundance in the environment. Despite the availability of various fluorimetric detection tools for Hg2+, including organic fluorophores and aptasensors, they often suffer from shortcomings like the utilization of expensive chemicals and toxic organic solvents, multi-step synthesis, sometimes with poor selectivity and low sensitivity. Whereas, biomass-derived fluorophores, such as carbon dots (CDs), present themselves as cost-effective and environmentally benign alternatives that exhibit comparable efficacy. Herein, we report a reaction-driven sensing assembly based on CDs, MnO2 nanosheets, and hydroquinone monothiocarbonate (HQTC) for the detection of Hg2+ ions, which relies on the formation of a CDs-MnO2 FRET-conjugate, resulting in the quenching of the intrinsic fluorescence of CDs. In a pseudochemodosimetric approach, the thiophilic nature of mercury was utilized for in-situ generation of the reducing species, hydroquinone from HQTC, resulting in the reduction of MnO2 nanosheets, the release of fluorescent CDs back to the solution. The low limit of detection (LOD) was achieved as 2 ppb (0.01 μM). The probe worked efficiently in real water samples like sea, river with good recovery of spiked Hg2+ and in some Indian ayurvedic medicines as well. Furthermore, solid-phase detection with sodium alginate beads demonstrated the ability of this cost-effective sensing assembly for onsite detection of Hg2+ ions.

head.ce:title: A carbon dots-MnO2 nanosheet-based turn-on pseudochemodosimeter as low-cost probe for selective detection of hazardous mercury ion contaminations in water

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, BITS-Pilani, K.K. Birla Goa Campus, NH 17B, Bypass Road, Zuarinagar, Sancoale, Goa 403726, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mercury is a highly hazardous element due to its profound toxicity and wide abundance in the environment. Despite the availability of various fluorimetric detection tools for Hg2+, including organic fluorophores and aptasensors, they often suffer from shortcomings like the utilization of expensive chemicals and toxic organic solvents, multi-step synthesis, sometimes with poor selectivity and low sensitivity. Whereas, biomass-derived fluorophores, such as carbon dots (CDs), present themselves as cost-effective and environmentally benign alternatives that exhibit comparable efficacy. Herein, we report a reaction-driven sensing assembly based on CDs, MnO2 nanosheets, and hydroquinone monothiocarbonate (HQTC) for the detection of Hg2+ ions, which relies on the formation of a CDs-MnO2 FRET-conjugate, resulting in the quenching of the intrinsic fluorescence of CDs. In a pseudochemodosimetric approach, the thiophilic nature of mercury was utilized for in-situ generation of the reducing species, hydroquinone from HQTC, resulting in the reduction of MnO2 nanosheets, the release of fluorescent CDs back to the solution. The low limit of detection (LOD) was achieved as 2 ppb (0.01 μM). The probe worked efficiently in real water samples like sea, river with good recovery of spiked Hg2+ and in some Indian ayurvedic medicines as well. Furthermore, solid-phase detection with sodium alginate beads demonstrated the ability of this cost-effective sensing assembly for onsite detection of Hg2+ ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury, a highly toxic heavy metal, is a global environmental concern due to its detrimental effects on human health and ecosystems [1–3]. Owing to its widespread use in various industrial activities, including coal combustion, mining, and manufacturing processes, as well as natural processes such as volcanic eruptions and weathering of rocks, mercury can be released into the environment [4–6]. Once released, mercury can undergo a series of transformations, leading to its bioaccumulation and biomagnification in the food chain, particularly in fish and seafood, posing a significant risk to human populations that rely on these food sources [7–9]. The accumulation of mercury via these contaminated food sources and industrial processes can lead to numerous detrimental effects, such as neurological disorders, kidney failure, cardiovascular disorders, and cognitive and motor coordination disorders [10,11]. Mercury contamination in water is a real concern in highly populated countries like India [12], as its citizens profusely take water-based food resources in their daily diet. Case studies suggested higher mercury contamination in oceans, rivers, estuaries (e.g. the Mandovi estuarine system, Goa), lakes (e.g.Vembanad lake of Kerala) etc. in India with mercury concentration notably high in both as sediment (upto 80.1 ppb) and suspended particulate matter (upto 537 ppb) [13,14]. Due to its acute toxicity, the USEPA has imposed a permissible limit for mercury as just 2 ppb in drinking water, whereas, WHO has set a limit of 50 ppb in soil for agriculture [15].

To address the potential hazards associated with mercury contamination, accurate and sensitive detection methods are essential for monitoring its levels in different environmental compartments and biological samples. Over the years, significant progress has been made in the development of analytical techniques for mercury detection, ranging from conventional methods to emerging technologies, such as atomic absorption spectrometry [16,17], cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry [18], inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry [19,20], electrochemical [21,22], voltammetric [23] and volumetric analysis [24]. These methods offer good sensitivity and selectivity but often require complex sample preparation and expensive equipment, limiting their accessibility and widespread use. On the other hand, colorimetric and fluorimetric methods are simple to handle, selective, and sensitive toward analytes. In this regard, several small organic fluorophores [25–28], gold and silver nanoparticles [29,30], silica nanoparticles [31,32], and even graphene-based sensors [33,34] have been developed for the detection of Hg2+ ions. Often these approaches make use of chemosensors that rely on complexation interactions or employ irreversible reaction-based chemodosimetric strategy to produce detectable signals upon interaction with Hg2+ ions [35,36]. However, multistep synthesis adds to the overall cost, the use of toxic organic solvent raises environmental concerns, and low sensitivity at times is disadvantageous, particularly for Hg2+ detection in biological samples.

In recent years, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) and MnO2 nanosheets have been individually established as useful functional nanomaterials with a plethora of applications [37–40]. CDs, owing to their excellent optical properties, good water-solubility, and exceptional biocompatibility, are extensively utilized as catalysts, organic light-emitting diodes, and sensors for various chemical species, anions, and metal ions [41–46]. Notably, a few nitrogen or sulphur-doped carbon dots-based fluorescent probes are available for the detection of mercury, however, these methods lack selectivity and are impaired by the heavy atom quenching effect [47–49]. On the other hand, the powerful oxidase-like property of MnO2 nanosheets (MnO2 NS) has garnered significant research attention in the generation of “turn-on” fluorescent sensors for numerous analytes [50–52]. MnO2 nanosheets with strong light absorption properties and negatively charged surfaces allow the occurrence of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) when coupled with strong donor species such as carbon nanodots. Therefore, utilizing a combination of CDs and MnO2 NS as FRET-based sensors for the detection of important chemical and biological species has gained preference over traditional methods [40]. The first generation of CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate-based detection systems relied on the retrieval of fluorescence by the release of carbon dots after the analyte (the reducing species) prompted the disintegration of MnO2 nanosheets. Based on this strategy, several fluorescent CDs-MnO2 NS FRET pair-based “turn-on” sensors for various reducing species like ascorbic acid (AA) [53,54], glutathione (GSH) [55,56], organophosphate (OP) [57], uric acid [58,59], and hydrazine [60] have been developed. In a notable advancement, a derivative of AA was used by Qu et. al., which is cleaved by alkaline phosphate (ALP) in-situ to generate AA that can subsequently release fluorescent CDs from the CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate and therefore, the sensing ensemble could act as a “turn-on” sensor for ALP [61]. In general, there are limited attempts at reaction-driven CDs-MnO2 NS sensing assembly in which a protected form of a reducing species is tapped by the analyte to release the fluorescent CDs in the solution. This may be attributed to the limited synthetic modality of species like AA. In the context of the study, a CDs-MnO2-based aptasensor for mercury detection is reported by Wei and co-workers, which involves the use of costly biospecies, DNA aptamers for labeling purposes [62] indicating the scope of the development of a CDs-MnO2 based cheaper alternative. Recently, a redox-relay reaction-based CDs-MnO2 sensor was developed by our group for the selective detection of E. coli utilizing hydroquinone (HQ)-benzoquinone (BQ) redox chemistry [63]. Inspired by this, we envisaged, in a pseudochemodosimetric approach, the installation of a mercury recognition unit to cheap and easily modifiable reducing species; HQ can offer a cost-effective CDs-MnO2-based sensing assembly for Hg2+ ions. The strategy partly resembles any chemodosimeter, which undergoes an analyte-driven reaction to generate the probe with an optical response, but in this case, the optical probe is unaltered. In the present study, HQ was converted to its monothiocarbonate derivative (HQTC), which is irreversibly converted to HQ by the thiophilic mercury, resulting in the disintegration of MnO2 nanosheets, liberating CDs into the solution and subsequently restoring their fluorescence. The CDs-MnO2NS-HQTC based pseudochemodosimeter is efficient and selective for the aqueous and solid-phase detection of inorganic mercury.

The potato (S. tuberosum) samples used in the synthesis of carbon dots were obtained from the local market in Vasco, Goa, India. Hydroquinone (95%) was procured from Molychem, India. Morpholinoethane sulphonic acid monohydrate (MES buffer, 99%) and potassium permanganate (KMnO4) were purchased from LOBA Chemie, Pvt. Ltd., India. All other chemicals and solvents utilized in the study were obtained from various commercial suppliers. The chemicals were of analytical grade and were used directly without undergoing any additional purification steps. The dialysis membrane-150 (cutoff 12000–14000 kDa, pore size 2.4 nm) was purchased from Himedia laboratories. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized Millipore water (18.2 MΩ·cm @ 25 °C).

All fluorescence measurements were carried out using a JASCO FP-8500 spectrofluorometer with an excitation wavelength set at 360 nm, and emission spectra were recorded in the range of 365–700 nm. The slit width was kept at 5 nm for both excitation and emission measurements. Infrared (IR) spectra were obtained using an IR Affinity-1 FTIR Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). UV-Vis spectra were acquired utilizing a JASCO V-550 spectrophotometer, with a bandwidth and data pitch set at 1 nm. The zeta potential and DLS study were carried out using Particulate system NanoPlus zeta/nano particle analyzer. The NMR spectra were obtained using a Bruker Avance Neo 500 MHz spectrometer. A Triple Quadrupole LC/MS (model 6460) was used to obtain the ESI-MS spectra. The field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) images and energy dispersive x-ray (EDAX) were obtained using a Quanta FEG 250 (FEI). A JOEL JEM 2100 F field emission gun-transmission electron microscope, operating at voltages between 120–200 kV, with a resolution of 1.9 Å was utilized to capture high resolution transmission electron micrograph (HRTEM) images. The X-ray diffraction studies were carried out using a Bruker D8 Advance powder X-ray diffractometer (XRD). Liquid and solid-phase images under normal and UV light were captured using a Mi10i camera (108 MP). The time-resolved fluorescence experiments were performed on Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorocube instrument fitted with a 375 nm diode laser excitation source.

In a 25 mL round-bottomed flask containing HQ (220 mg, 2 mmol) dissolved in methyl tert-butyl ether (MTBE, 5 mL) N,N-diisopropylethylamine (DIPEA) (568.5 mg, 2.2 mmol) was added, and the resulting mixture was stirred at room temperature under nitrogen atmosphere. After 5 min, O-phenyl chlorothionoformate (414 mg, 1.1 mmol) was added in a dropwise manner at ice-cold conditions, the mixture was allowed to reach room temperature, and the reaction mixture was stirred for another 30 min. Upon completion of the reaction, the organic layer was washed with 5% NaHCO3, then with water, and it was passed through anhydrous Na2SO4, evaporated to dryness, yielding a crude product mixture. It was subsequently purified by column chromatography to afford pure HQTC (407 mg, 91%) as an off-white solid. 1H NMR (500 MHz, CDCl3): δ 8.79 (s, 1 H), 7.42 (t, J = 8.3 Hz, 2 H), 7.32–7.27 (m, 1 H), 7.18 (t, J = 8.5 Hz, 2 H), 6.99 (d, J = 9.1 Hz, 2 H), 6.87 (d, J = 9.1 Hz, 2 H); 13C NMR (125 MHz, CDCl3): δ 195.3, 155.6, 153.4, 146.1, 129.5, 126.4, 122.2, 121.7, 116.0.

The carbon dots were synthesized via hydrothermal treatment of potato pulp (S. tuberosum) as per the reported procedure [64]. In brief, ground potato pulp was autoclaved and heated at 200 °C for 12 h to afford a dark brown solution. The resultant solution was filtered, centrifuged, and dialyzed against deionized water for 12 h by changing water after every 2 h. The resulting solution was freeze-dried (−10 oC, 50 Pa, 8 h), re-suspended in water to prepare a stock solution of 1 mg/mL, and stored at 4 °C for subsequent analytical studies.

Adopting a reported procedure, the synthesis of MnO2 nanosheets was carried out by the reduction of aqueous KMnO4 in MES buffer via sonication at room temperature for 30 min [65]. The resulting brown-colored solution was centrifuged, washed with deionized water three times, and freeze-dried (−10 oC, 50 Pa, 6 h) to get MnO2 nanosheets. The solid was re-suspended in water to prepare a stock solution for subsequent analytical investigations.

Sodium alginate (250 mg) was dissolved in 10 mL of HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) by vigorous stirring for 90 min at room temperature [66]. Separately, CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate was prepared by adding 2 mL of CDs solution (1 mg/mL) and 3.6 mL of stock MnO2 nanosheets solution. The CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate was added to the alginate solution and the mixture was degassed via sonication and refrigerated at 4 oC for 6 h. The CDs-MnO2 containing alginate mixture was slowly dropped using a pipette into a slow-stirring CaCl2 solution (2.5% w/v). The beads were removed after 2 min, rinsed with water, and stored under deionized water at 4 oC for further use.

A 4 mM stock solution of Hg2+ ions was prepared in water and utilized for all fluorimetric studies. The sensing ensemble contained 100 μL of CDs (1 mg/mL), 180 μL of MnO2 (1 mg/mL), and 5 μL HQTC (4 mM in THF), followed by dilution with water to 1 mL total volume. For the detection of mercury ions, an increasing volume of aqueous solution of Hg2+ was added to the sensing solution keeping the volume constant (1 mL), the solution was incubated for 15 min before checking the fluorescence output. In order to evaluate the selectivity of sensing assay towards Hg2+ ions stock solutions of various cations and anions (1 mM) were prepared in water. All studies were performed at room temperature, triplicated and the average data was reported.

A) Water samples: For real sample analysis, water from various sources such as tap, sea, river, field, pond, and rain were collected. To 0.5 mL of real water samples, stock solutions of CDs, MnO2 nanosheets and HQTC were added. The mixture was spiked with known concentrations of Hg2+ was added and the final volume was made up to 1 mL by the addition of real water samples and subjected to fluorimetric evaluation.

B) Ayurvedic medicines: Triphala and kayam powders from two different companies were purchased from the local market and used for investigation purposes. For sample preparation, 1 g of each powder was suspended in 5 mL of nitric acid, and stirred at 80 °C for 1 h to convert all mercurals into its soluble nitrate salts. The resulting mixture was filtered and neutralized. 1 mL of the aliquot was diluted by HEPES buffer to make 10 mL of stock solution. The real sample analysis was carried out by adding varying amounts (100 μL and 200 μL) of the stock solutions to the sensing system. Similarly, for spiking purpose, 100 μL of the stock solution was taken and spiked by the known concentration of Hg2+, and the same solution was added to the sensing system, and the final volume was adjusted to 1 mL before fluorimetric analysis.

The sensing studies on the solid phase were carried out using sodium alginate beads infused with CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate. The alginate beads were dipped in 1 mL solutions with varying concentrations of Hg2+ ions containing 50 μL of HQTC (4 mM) for 2 h. The fluorimetric change was captured by a mobile camera and subjected to solid-phase fluorimetric image analysis of CTCF values using ImageJ analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design, synthesis and characterization of the sensing probe
ce:section.ce:para: In our endeavour to develop a carbon-based cost-effective detection system for Hg2+ ions, we planned to utilize the thiophilicity of mercury ions in the design of a reaction-driven CDs-MnO2-based sensing assembly. Toward this objective, the easily available hydroquinone was chosen as the reducing species and a thiocarbonate group as the mercury recognition unit. Hydroquinone was treated with O-phenyl chlorothionoformate in the presence of DIPEA to obtain hydroquinone monothiocarbonate (HQTC). The formation of the probe was characterized by 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectroscopy. The 1H NMR spectrum exhibited six distinct sets of peaks, which indicate the formation of a monoprotected product of HQ. In the aromatic region (δ 6.5–7.5 ppm), the probe molecule HQTC displayed two doublets at δ 6.89 and 6.99 ppm, which correspond to the aromatic protons of the 1,4-substituted phenyl ring of HQTC and three peaks at δ 7.18, 7.29, and 7.42 ppm corresponded to the protons of the second aromatic ring (Fig. S1). The presence of a peak at δ 8.79 ppm corresponds to the available phenolic -OH group confirming the selective mono-protection of hydroquinone. In the 13C NMR spectrum, the presence of a peak at δ 195.3 ppm signifies the characteristic peak of CS group of thiocarbonate functionality (Fig. S2). The NMR data collectively confirmed the successful formation of HQTC. The pseudochemodosimeter showed moderate water solubility of 1 mg per 10 mL (0.4 mM) at room temperature.

In a simple way, the biomass-derived blue carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of potato pulp. The successful formation of CDs was indicated by the distinctive UV-Vis spectrum, displaying a prominent peak at 310 nm (Fig. S3a) and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum, which showed the presence of expected surface functionalities (Fig. S3b). The formation of CDs was further confirmed by FESEM and EDAX analysis, which showed a strong spike for elemental carbon and no nitrogen or other elements, a characteristic of potato-derived CDs (Fig. S3c,d). As carbon dots display excitation-dependent emission, the CDs were excited at different wavelengths and their emission intensities were recorded ( Fig. 1a). The study showed that upon excitation at 360 nm, the carbon dots exhibited a strong blue emission (λem 445 nm) with a quantum yield of 16.5%, with respect to quinine sulphate as the standard [67]. The HRTEM image of CDs displayed uniform morphology with a near-spherical shape of 7 nm average diameter and excellent dispersion (Fig. 1b). The generation of MnO2 nanosheets was achieved by simple reduction of KMnO4 by MES buffer and its formation was established by the presence of a characteristic broad absorption band spanning from 300 to 500 nm in UV-Vis spectroscopy and characteristic peaks in the XRD spectrum (Fig. S4a,b). The formation of MnO2 NS was further confirmed by FESEM images which showed the sheet-like morphology along with EDAX analysis where the presence of a high amount of elemental Mn was observed (Fig. S4c,d). As expected, a significant overlap in the absorption spectrum of MnO2 NS and emission spectra of CDs was observed, indicating the possibility of a FRET between them (Fig. 1c). Upon addition of MnO2 nanosheets in the aqueous solution of CDs, electrostatic interactions led to adsorption of CDs onto the surface of nanosheets to produce CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate. The fluorescence intensity of the CDs (100 μL, 1 mg/L) decreased by 87.5% upon the addition of 180 μL of MnO2 (1 mg/mL), and upon further addition, the fluorescence was completely quenched (Fig. 1d). To investigate the electrostatic interactions within the nanoconjugate, the zeta potential values of CDs, MnO2 nanosheets, and the CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate were measured. The pristine CDs with a negatively charged surface exhibited a high negative zeta potential value of − 23.75 mV, whereas, the MnO2 nanosheets displayed a relatively lower zeta potential of − 17.15 mV. When MnO2 nanosheets were added to the CDs solution a decrease in the zeta potential to − 18.30 mV was observed. The change in the zeta potential value strongly indicates that CDs engaged in electrostatic interaction with the surface of MnO2 nanosheets. Additionally, EDAX analysis of the mixture displayed the presence of elemental Mn along with carbon and oxygen and the FESEM image showed a compacted sheet-like structure indicating the generation of CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate (Fig. S5). The EDAX color mapping analysis showed that CDs (purple) are evenly distributed on the surface of MnO2 nanosheets (red) indicating the formation of CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate (Fig. S6). The formation of the nanoconjugate was further confirmed by HRTEM images, while the MnO2 nanosheets revealed a sheet-like morphology (Fig. 1e), and CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate revealed a densely packed sheet-like morphology, signifying the adherence of CDs to MnO2 nanosheets (Fig. 1f).

For the design and development of a pseudochemodosimetric fluorimetric sensor for Hg2+ ions, we initially tested the viability of our CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate against hydroquinone. The formation of CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate led to the quenching of the inherent fluorescence of the CDs through FRET [40]. However, the gradual addition of HQ led to the restoration of the blue fluorescence of the system, indicating its ability to reduce MnO2 nanosheets and thereby releasing the CDs to the solution. The complete restoration of the fluorescence was noted upon the addition of 200 μM of HQ (Fig. S7). A similar trend was observed when the CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate was incubated with 200 μM of HQTC and increasing concentrations of Hg2+ ions ( Fig. 2), and the revival of the fluorescence was proportional to the amount of Hg2+ ions (Fig. S8a). The plot of the change of fluorescence output with a concentration bore a sigmoidal curve with a saturation point at 150 μM of Hg2+, which is similar to HQ and corresponds to a high regression coefficient (R
2 = 0.9971) (Fig. S8b). Next, the sensing ability of the CDs-MnO2-HQTC sensing ensemble was evaluated as a function of pH (3−10) to identify the most suited pH for the detection of Hg2+ ions. The sensing ensemble displayed negligible fluorescence output in the absence of Hg2+ ions across the whole range of the pH (Fig. S9). Upon introduction of Hg2+ ions, the CDs-MnO2 sensing platform displayed good fluorescence response in the pH range 6–8, with the highest response at near neutral pH (pH 7.4). Presumably, a strong acidic medium is not suitable for the reduction of MnO2 NS by HQ, while in a strong basic medium, the formation of Hg(OH)2 may inhibit the sensing process. Based on the preliminary studies, the detection was carried out in the aqueous solution (pH 7) and at a lower concentration range of Hg2+ (0–20 μM) by incubating CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate with 20 μM of HQTC as a significant turn-on response was observed within that range to get a standard plot.

In order to ensure sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions, the sensing ensemble consisting of CDs (100 μL, 1 mg/L), MnO2 NS (180 μL, 1 mg/L) and HQTC (5 μL, 4 mM) was incubated with increasing amounts of Hg2+ ions (0–24 μM) for 15 min before measuring the fluorescence output. In the absence of mercury ions, the ensemble displayed minimal fluorescence, however, the incremental addition of Hg2+ ions led to a gradual restoration of the blue fluorescence of CDs at λmax 445 nm. A time-scale study revealed that 15 min incubation with Hg2+ ions is enough for an in-situ generation of HQ to express the highest fluorescence from the sensing solution (Fig. S10). Overall, a 3-folds increase of fluorescence was observed and as expected, the saturation point was reached upon the addition of 20 μM of Hg2+ ions indicating its concentration is proportional to HQTC concentration ( Fig. 3a,b). The fluorescence output exhibited a linear relationship in the lower concentration range of 0.01–0.1 μM, of Hg2+ with high regression coefficient (R 2 = 0.9906), and the limit of detection was considered as 0.01 μM or 2 ppb as the lowest detectable point in the lower concentration vs relative intensity plot (Fig. 3c).

During the course of our studies, we noticed that as the MnO2 nanosheets were reduced, the brownish colour of the solution slowly faded, indicating the simple combination of MnO2 NS-HQTC can perform as a colorimetric sensor for Hg2+ ions as well. HQTC has a UV-Vis band at around 240 nm (Fig. S11a). Similarly, HQ showed a band at a similar region and the major band of the corresponding oxidized product BQ appeared at 276 nm (Fig. S11b,c). Therefore, it does not overlap with the strong band of MnO2 NS. Initially, the absorption spectrum of the MnO2-HQTC was recorded, which gave a similar absorption peak as that of MnO2 nanosheets, and the small additional peak comes from HQTC (Fig. S11d). Upon incubation with Hg2+ ions (0–24 μM), a gradual decrease in the absorption peak was noted, with saturation again occurring at 20 μM of Hg2+ ions ( Fig. 4a). The corresponding decrease displayed a linear trend from 1–10 μM and from the graph the detection limit was determined to be 1.0 μM (0.2 ppm, Fig. 4b). The system displayed excellent selectivity against other cations and anions indicating the viable use of the MnO2 NS-HQTC combination as a colorimetric sensor for mercury ions (Fig. S12). Therefore, this material-based pseudochemodosimiter using HQTC as the probe can perform as a dual detection tool (both fluorimetric and colorimetric) for Hg2+ ions.

In order to comprehensively evaluate the selectivity of the CDs-MnO2-HQTC ensemble-based fluorescence assay towards Hg2+ ions, a series of tests was conducted against a diverse range of cations, anions, and interfering small molecules. Fig. 5 illustrates the response of the CDs-MnO2-HQTC based sensing system towards a range of common cations (Ag+, Al3+, Ca2+, Cd2+, etc.), anions (F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, SCN−, etc.), small molecules including urea, oxidising agents (e.g·H2O2) and common amino acids. Notably, the CDs-MnO2 assembly demonstrated negligible fluorescence fluctuations in the presence of these potentially interfering species. On the contrary, the introduction of Hg2+ ions resulted in a significant fluorescence enhancement, indicating the excellent selectivity towards Hg2+ ions in the presence of several common cations, anions, amino acids, etc.

The CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate is previously used as turn-on sensors for reducing species like ascorbic acid, glutathione, and cysteine [53–56]. Expectedly, these species are prominent interfering agents in the current sensing system (CDs-MnO2-HQTC) for Hg2+ detection. A short study with the above-mentioned species was carried out using CDs-MnO2-HQTC assembly in the presence and absence of Hg2+ (Fig. S13). All three reducing species could actually produce a significant response when individually added to CDs-MnO2 NS-HQTC solution under the standard sensing condition. However, the co-existence of such reducing species with mercury ions in natural abundance is highly unlikely. Moreover, Hg2+ ions have a stronger affinity towards thiol functionality than thiocarbonate and therefore, it could easily indulge in complex formation with Cys or GSH. In fact, when the sensing ensemble co-incubated with Hg2+ ions and GSH/Cys it failed to produce any turn-on response, which in-turn indicated that mercury was no longer in the ionic state to perform the cleavage of the thiocarbonate group of HQTC. It also indicated that the present sensing system is not suitable for bioaccumulated mercury. It is known that nitrite ions can spontaneously react with AA and oxidize it to an inactive form [68]. As nitrite is inert to the sensing system, we thought, it can be used to combat the effect of AA, if at all it co-exists with mercury. A competitive experiment with 1 equiv of AA (20 μM) and Hg2+ (20 μM) in the presence of NO2 − (30 μM) could affect a similar turn-on response, whereas, only AA and NO2 − in the solution could hardly release any CDs to the solution (Fig. S13). As such, the present sensing system is highly selective to inorganic mercury ions in the presence of various cations, anions and several small molecules, which is attributed to the unique thiophilicity of Hg2+ leading to the cleavage of monothiocarbonate group of HQTC to generate HQ in-situ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability and stability studies of the sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: As CDs are released into the solution after MnO2 nanosheets is reduced by the Hg2+ ions mediated release of HQ, it is expected that the carbon dots can be reused for mercury detection with the re-fuelling of MnO2 NS and HQTC in the sensing solution. To study reusability, MnO2 NS and HQTC were added to the sensing solution after the first cycle and a second cycle of detection was carried out. It displayed a fluorescence turn-on response with a moderate drop in the threshold value, indicating the possible reusability of CDs (Fig. S14). However, on the third cycle, only about 55% retrieval of the original fluorescence intensity was noted which can be attributed to the increase in the volume of the solution, and a large increase of interfering by-products like Mn2+, HQ, BQ in the same solution (Fig. S14). Although reusability is not recommended, the use of cheap and easily available components in the sensing assembly and greener attributes largely compensate for it. Unlike organic fluorophores, CDs show good rack stability, and no photobleaching properties. Practically, the CDs both in solid and solution state was unperturbed in terms of fluorescence output when monitored for 6 months. Similarly, good rack stability was shown both by MnO2 NS and HQTC was stable at 4 °C for several months providing reproducible results.

Before proceeding further, a comparative assessment of the CDs-MnO2-HQTC sensing ensemble with other available methods in terms of relative synthetic cost, hazards, sensitivity, and selectivity was carried out with selected small molecule-based chemodosimeters and several other carbon material-based sensors for Hg2+ (Table S1, S2). As seen in table S1, the organic probes were engaged in the use of toxic solvents and reagents during the course of their multistep syntheses and sensing processes, and therefore, these are neither cost-effective nor environmentally benign. The available carbon material-based sensors use costly ligands or doping agents for surface modifications which add to the overall cost (Table S2). A quick cost calculation revealed that the current CDs-MnO2-HQTC assembly cost is about INR 40 per 100 mg of the CDs and the proportionate amount of MnO2 and HQTC, whereas, any other small molecule probe is 10–100 folds (even more) costlier. Notably, 100 mg of CDs can be used for 1000 analyses and thus the cost per analysis is less than one-twentieth of the Indian rupee. The current sensing system can outperform several of the existing protocols in terms of smart strategy, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendly attributes.

The sensing mechanism of the present system relies on the Hg2+ mediated in-situ generation of the reducing species HQ, which disintegrates the MnO2 nanosheets, leading to the release of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) back into the solution. Mercury is highly thiophilic in nature, therefore, when introduced into the system, it spontaneously deprotects the thiocarbonate group of HQTC, resulting in the formation of HQ and converting itself to HgS ( Fig. 6). The HQ immediately reduces the MnO2 nanosheets to water-soluble Mn2+, releasing the CDs and restoring its intense blue fluorescence. The proposed sensing mechanism of FRET-conjugate formation and mercury-driven disintegration of MnO2 NS to release CDs was indicated by DLS study, zeta potential analysis and fluorescence lifetime measurements. In DLS study, the average size of CDs was observed in the expected range (7 nm) with a narrow distribution, and MnO2 nanosheets showed an average size of 212 nm. Upon formation of the CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate, the average size showed a nominal change to 225 nm, however, after the addition of HQTC and Hg2+ ions a significant reduction of the average size (42 nm) in the sensing solution was observed indicating rupture of MnO2 nanosheets to smaller units allowing the release of CDs into the solution (Fig. S15, Table S3). The FRET conjugate formation between CDs and MnO2 NS is already established by zeta potential analysis; upon the addition of Hg2+ the zeta potential showed a significant decrease to − 9.32 mV (from −18.30 mV), indicating the generation of positive ions (here Mn2+) in the solution. Further, the fluorescence lifetime measurements of pristine carbon dots, CDs-MnO2 NS conjugates and HQTC with and without the addition of Hg2+ ions were conducted (Fig. S16). CDs initially exhibited a multiexponential decay profile with an average lifetime of 44.78 ns. Upon the introduction of MnO2 nanosheets, the decay of the CDs' excited state accelerated and the average lifetime of the CDs decreased to around 33.42 ns (Fig. S16, Table S4). This reduction in lifetime aligns with the concept of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) as the quenching mechanism in CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate. As HQTC is a nonfluorescent and neutral molecule, it does not take part in the decay process and therefore, τavg of the sensing assembly (CDs-MnO2-HQTC) remained same (34.40 ns, Table S4, SI). Upon the addition of variable amounts of Hg2+ in the solution, the CDs decay profile was expectedly recovered (τavg 43.89). The fluorescence lifetime measurement experiments firmly indicate the FRET-conjugate based sensing process. To confirm the generation of HQ from HQTC, a solution containing HQTC (20 µM), and Hg2+ ions (10 µM), which would allow a partial reaction, was subjected to ESI-MS analysis. As expected, the ESI-MS spectra showed a molecular ion peak of m/z 111.0 at RT 1.52 min indicating the generation of HQ [M+ 1] and the molecular ion peak of unreacted HQTC (m/z 247.1, [M+1]) at RT 5.36 in the solution firmly confirming the Hg2+ mediated spontaneous cleavage of thiocarbonate group of HQTC (Fig. S17). The experimental facts thoroughly suggest the pseudochemodosimeter approach using HQTC as the probe is effective in developing CDs-MnO2 NS based sensing tool for Hg2+.

Several reports indicated mercury contamination in various natural water bodies indicating its estimation and remediation are of utmost importance [3,7,12–14]. To illustrate the practical ability of the CDs-MnO2-HQTC based sensing ensemble in real-world applications, first, water samples from various sources such as pond, river, sea, tap, lake, and rain were collected and spiked with predetermined concentrations of Hg2+ ions. The spiked samples were then incubated with CDs-MnO2-HQTC sensing assay and their fluorescence output was recorded. All data were triplicated and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was within 3–4% signifying the reproducibility of the data. The %recovery for all real samples was above 95% indicating the efficient functioning of the sensing assay in the detection of mercury ions in real water samples ( Table 1). Notably, seawater, collected from one of the beaches of south Goa, India showed a recovery % above 100% in variable spiked concentrations of Hg2+ probably indicating the low-level presence of inorganic mercury in that water sample, however, the mercury contamination is within the permissible limit and below the detection limit of sensing assembly as the fluorescence response in the same water sample without spiking was negligible.

To further explore the applicability of the sensing assembly, various Indian ayurvedic medicines were selected. Several Indian ayurvedic medicines contain mercury in the form of its sulphide [69]. In this direction, triphala and kayam powders of different makes were first digested by nitric acid, the resulting solution was neutralized and diluted by HEPES buffer. The sample solutions were subjected to analysis using CDs-MnO2-HQTC sensing ensemble and the results were tabulated in Table S5. It may be noticed that triphala samples showed measurable amounts of mercury contamination (up to 40 μg/g) but kayam powders have negligible (or below the detection limit) presence of mercury. The study contemplates the potential utility of the CDs-MnO2 based sensing probe in real-world applications.

For on-site use of the sensing tool, it is convenient to use a probe-modified solid-phase for the detection of analytes. We have demonstrated the solid-phase detection study for CDs-MnO2-HQTC system for the detection of Hg2+ ions in sodium alginate beads as the solid platform ( Fig. 7a). The sodium alginate beads (2% w/v) loaded with CDs-MnO2 NS were dipped in a solution of HQTC (200 μM) and varying concentrations of Hg2+ ions (0–200 μM). The CDs-MnO2 alginate beads upon incubation for 2 h regained fluorescence at different levels depending on the concentration of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 7b). The increase in fluorescence was found to be proportional to the concentration of Hg2+ ions. The count of total fluorescence (CTCF) was plotted against the concentration of Hg2+ ions and a saturation curve was found to occur at 60 μM, which indicated the utilization of the CDs-MnO2 alginate beads as a viable assay for the detection of Hg2+ ions (Fig. 7c). The sigmoidal curve of CTCF values against the concentration of Hg2+ showed a high regression coefficient of R
2 = 0.9935. The solid-phase assay was able to detect Hg2+ ions at a reasonably low concentration of 5 μM.

The current work demonstrates a pseudochemodosimetric approach by masking the reducing species hydroquinone with a mercury recognition unit to afford a cost-effective CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate based sensing protocol for the detection of inorganic mercury in aqueous media. For CDs-MnO2 nanoconjugate, upon mixing electron-rich CDs with good electron acceptor MnO2 nanosheets, its inherent fluorescence was lost by the FRET mechanism. As per the strategy, the thiophilic nature of mercury was exploited for in-situ generation of the reducing species, HQ from the probe hydroquinone monothiocarbonate to result in the disintegration of MnO2 nanosheets followed by the release of blue fluorescent CDs into the solution. Each component of the sensing assembly and the sensing event were characterized by NMR, mass, SEM, HRTEM, time-resolved spectroscopy, DLS, etc. Hg2+ bears a linear relationship with the concentration of HQTC and a linear rise in fluorescence was obtained with R 2 = 0.9906 at a lower concentration range (0.01–0.1 μM) from which the LOD was estimated as 2 ppb (0.01 μM). The sensing platform exhibits marked sensitivity and excellent selectivity towards mercury ions in a competitive environment in the presence of several metal ions, anions, amino acids and even various reducing and oxidizing species. The components of the sensing system showed rack stability for months and a quick cost calculation revealed that each analysis costs below one-twentieth of the Indian rupees. The excellent recovery of spiked Hg2+ in various real water samples by CDs-MnO2 NS-HQTC is demonstrated with excellent %recovery. Notably, the sensing ensemble could efficiently detect mercury contamination in Indian ayurvedic medicine as well. The solid-phase detection of mercury in the sensing probe loaded alginate beads is a prominent attribute of its possible on-site applications. The use of biomass-derived CDs, cheap chemicals like KMnO4 and hydroquinone, easy and hazardless synthesis of sensing components, sensing in aqueous media, and high selectivity and sensitivity are some of the merits of the current environmentally benign protocol which supersede many existing methods of the detection of Hg2+ ions. Finally, the strategy of installation of a recognition unit to cheap and easily modifiable reducing species, hydroquinone can in principle be extended to other analytes in developing CDs-MnO2 based sensing tools for them, and further exploration of this pseudochemodosimeter approach is currently undertaken in our laboratory.

Mercury is toxic even at ppb level and adds havoc to environmental pollution. Its use is still prevalent in several industries such as coal mining. Following its release into the environment mercury undergoes biomagnification, accumulating in the food chain causing toxicity to the environment and animals. Mercury contamination in drinking or irrigation water is a serious health hazard to humans. The low-level detection of mercury ions is therefore crucial in different environmental compartments including water. The other side in disguise is the hazards associated with the synthesis and use of several sensing protocols for mercury. Although organic probes are used in small quantities for analytical purposes, the use of multiple toxic reagents and organic solvents during synthesis as well as sensing events vastly creates environmental hazards. The benign use of biomass, harmless reagents and avoidance of organic solvent is a path forward for sustainability and the current work is an attempt in that direction.

A.T. performed the analytical experiments, interpreted the results and writing of the manuscript. A.A.B. synthesized the final probe required for detection. S.D.H. prepared the carbon dots required for the analysis. M.B. engaged with scientific inputs, manuscript writing and reviewing. A.C. inception of the concept and management of the whole project.

Sharanabasava D. Hiremath: Formal analysis, Methodology. AMRITA CHATTERJEE: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Project administration, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
